viewerQuestion: How can you verify that an AI Agent really completed a task instead of trusting a polished completion message?
centralThesis: Reliable AI Agent acceptance defines a fair task before execution, verifies the final environment state with the right grader mix, repeats trials to measure consistency, and audits the evaluation itself before making a release decision.
reusableArtifact: AI Agent Acceptance Sheet: Outcome, Task Setup, Evidence, Grader Mix, Reliability, Eval Health, Exceptions, Release Decision

P0: A completion message is a claim, not outcome evidence
Final-state verification and explicit success criteria are the core acceptance gate
The reusable acceptance sheet records evidence and a release decision
P1: Separate task, trial, grader, transcript, outcome, and harness
Combine deterministic, model-based, and human graders by dimension
Run multiple isolated trials and choose the reliability metric by product need
Audit task ambiguity, reference solutions, graders, transcripts, and suite health
P2: Partial credit and avoiding overfitting to one tool path
Real failures as initial dataset seeds
Production monitoring and human review as complementary layers
P3Removed: Long benchmark examples that do not change the acceptance method
Repeated explanations of the same grader trade-off

chapters.label: Introduction
chapters.promise: See why an AI Agent completion message is only a claim

chapters.label: Define Success
chapters.promise: Define a fair task and distinguish transcript from outcome

chapters.label: Grade the Outcome
chapters.promise: Inspect final state with the right grader mix

chapters.label: Test Reliability
chapters.promise: Use repeated trials to measure consistency

chapters.label: Audit the Eval
chapters.promise: Check whether the evaluation itself is fair

chapters.label: Acceptance Sheet
chapters.promise: Capture evidence, exceptions, and the release decision

visualSemantics.humanRole: defines outcomes, acceptance criteria, grader boundaries, and release decisions
visualSemantics.agentRole: executes tasks, leaves traces, changes final state, and participates in repeated trials
visualSemantics.artifact: one acceptance clipboard with a result state, grader mix, trial history, and release gate

factBoundaries: A correct final state does not automatically imply safe or efficient behavior
A deterministic grader can reject valid variants if the task is overspecified
One successful trial does not prove consistent reliability
A low score may reveal a broken task or grader rather than weak Agent capability

contentEdit.keptEvidence: All P0 and P1 content plus source evidence that explains mechanisms, boundaries, transfer, or credibility
contentEdit.removedOnly: Framework catalogs, acknowledgements, repeated examples, and details that add no new fact, boundary, step, or understanding value
